The Man Who
Refused
To Wait

How Ola Atose built Africa's most audacious regulated digital asset infrastructure — brick by brick, battle by battle — and why the continent will never be the same.

Scroll to read the story
Chapter I  —  The Wound That Wouldn't Close

A $250 Billion African Crypto Market — Nigeria at the Centre

Africa is not short of money. It is short of infrastructure that moves money with dignity. Every year, the continent bleeds billions through broken rails — remittances eaten alive by 8–12% fees, millions of citizens locked out of the global digital economy, and entrepreneurs forced to build on sand because the ground itself was never properly laid.

The numbers are staggering in their quiet indictment: over 350 million unbanked adults across Sub-Saharan Africa. an African crypto market estimated at $250 billion, with Nigeria alone processing approximately $92 billion and growing at 56% year-on-year. A regulatory vacuum that simultaneously criminalised and ignored an entire generation of financial builders.

Someone had to walk into that room. Someone had to say: not on my watch.

$250B
African crypto market opportunity
$92B
Nigeria transaction volume — 56% annual growth

Bank-integrated distribution reach through Nigerian partnerships

Nigeria + Ghana regulatory normalisation
Chapter II  —  The Making of a Market Builder

Ola Atose — Built in the Markets

Ola Atose is the Founder and Global CEO of KoinKoin, a pan-African digital asset infrastructure platform positioned at the intersection of regulated financial markets and blockchain-based value transfer. He brings over a decade of experience across global investment banking, electronic trading, and financial market infrastructure, with a track record of building and scaling institutional-grade platforms in complex regulatory environments.

Prior to founding KoinKoin, Ola held senior roles across Deutsche Bank, VTB Capital, Scotiabank, NatWest Markets, and Crédit Agricole — spanning electronic trading, market structure, and data-driven execution, including serving as Lead Portfolio Manager and Global Head of Electronic Trading & Data. This was not academic theory. It was live-market discipline: execution quality, controls, data, regulation, liquidity, and infrastructure under pressure.

That experience matters because Africa does not need another crypto app. It needs market infrastructure. It needs regulated rails, bank-integrated distribution, compliance visibility, institutional liquidity, and the confidence of regulators who have seen too many shortcuts. KoinKoin was built by someone who understands how real financial markets are supposed to work.

Under Ola's leadership, KoinKoin has become the only African-built exchange with dual-country regulatory normalisation across Nigeria and Ghana — with active engagement across central banks, securities regulators, and financial intelligence units, and the embedding of institutional-grade AML/CFT frameworks and real-time regulatory reporting infrastructure.

He has secured bank-integrated distribution partnerships reaching over 50 million users and positioned KoinKoin as a licensed on/off-ramp infrastructure provider for global exchanges, with inbound institutional demand supporting multi-billion-dollar monthly transaction flows. After eight years of honing, readiness, compliance work, and commercial positioning, KoinKoin is no longer preparing for the big move. It is steady for it.

"This is what eight years of preparation looks like: regulatory normalisation, bank distribution, institutional rails, and a founder who has already lived inside the machinery of global markets."

In the Arena

Chapter III  —  The Grind No One Sees

A Thousand Days of No

The romanticised version of the startup story skips the middle. It cuts from "I had an idea" to "we raised the round." What it omits is everything: the sleepless architecture decisions, the regulatory meetings where the room looked at you like you had invented a new language, the partners who said yes and then said nothing, the months when the account balance looked more like a countdown than a balance sheet.

Building regulated digital asset infrastructure in West Africa required Ola to simultaneously be a fintech entrepreneur, a regulatory diplomat, a technology architect, and a commercial deal-maker — across multiple jurisdictions, in real time. Nigeria and Ghana do not share a central bank, a licensing regime, or a unified crypto policy. Building compliantly in both, simultaneously, while also establishing a holding company under Abu Dhabi Global Market standards, is not a weekend project.

Most would have simplified. Ola chose the harder path: build the full architecture first, because that is the only architecture worth building. Shortcuts in infrastructure become crises at scale.

The Foundation
KoinKoin is incorporated. An ADGM holding structure is established — a signal not of ambition but of inevitability. This was never going to be a local play.
The Licences
Two sandboxes. Two regulators. Two wins. CBN AML/CFT/CPF Supervision Pilot (Nigeria) and SEC Ghana VASP Sandbox — one of the only platforms on the continent to hold dual regulated status.
The Infrastructure Stack
Five layers, fully integrated: on/off-ramp, OTC, stablecoin rails, custody, and compliance. Each layer built to institutional grade.
The Strategic Alliances
OPay. Wema ALAT. Binance. ChainUp. Live integration partners across the ecosystem. A 90-day Binance engagement plan active across both markets.
The Capital Raise
$100 million seed round. $333 million pre-money valuation. Infrastructure already built, licences already won, and a market that has been waiting decades for this platform to exist — setting up a targeted $600 million Series A by Q4 2027 and an almost $2 billion valuation by Q3 2028.
Chapter IV  —  You Cannot Build a Continent Alone

The People Behind the Platform

Every great company is, at its core, a great team. Ola understood early that the scale of the problem he was solving required a bench of people who were not just talented but genuinely motivated by the mission. KoinKoin's founding team spans compliance, technology, product, and operations — drawn from across the African diaspora and the global financial services world.

Mimi Kufuor. Jeremy Dobrin. Paul Olukoya. Adetayo Omidiran. Patience Omogbon. Nonso Nkeonye. Oyindamola Alabi. Mobolaji Ogundare. Margaret Odoro. These are not hired hands. These are believers. Each one chose to be here, chose the harder path, chose to bet their professional reputation on an infrastructure company that most of the traditional finance world had not yet caught up with.

People build this. Exceptional people who have chosen to be exceptional in this place, at this time.

"The best teams are not assembled. They are attracted — drawn in by a problem worth solving and a leader worth following."

The KoinKoin culture
Chapter V  —  Against the Current

Every Empire Was Once Impossible

Entrepreneurship extracts a price that does not appear on any balance sheet. The phone calls at 2am when a compliance flag appears. The investor who loved the deck last Tuesday and has gone quiet this Tuesday. The regulatory goalposts that move as you approach them.

Africa has a particular kind of entrepreneurial resistance. It is not malicious — it is structural. Decades of underdeveloped financial infrastructure, risk-averse legacy banking, and a global investment community that has historically undervalued African founders have created an environment where building anything at scale requires extraordinary resilience. Not grit as a personality trait. Grit as a survival mechanism.

The CBN AML/CFT supervision pilot — a landmark regulatory moment for the entire Nigerian VASP sector — was earned, alongside Flutterwave, Paystack, Juicyway, cNGN, and KuCoin, through years of regulatory engagement, compliance investment, and demonstrating that digital assets could be operated responsibly in Nigeria. That is not luck. That is years of showing up.

6
VASPs named in CBN pilot — KoinKoin among them
2
Jurisdictions, fully regulated: Nigeria + Ghana
ADGM
HoldCo domicile — Abu Dhabi Global Market
90
Days: live Binance integration plan, both markets

Africa
Doesn't Need
Permission

The world's fastest-growing economic bloc. The youngest population on earth. A digital-native generation that has never known a world without a mobile phone. The only thing Africa has been waiting for is infrastructure worthy of its ambition. KoinKoin is that infrastructure.

Chapter VI  —  What Comes Next

A Changed, More Powerful Africa

The returns table tells a part of the story: early investors in KoinKoin are entering at a $333 million pre-money valuation, with a clear path toward a $600 million Series A by Q4 2027 and an almost $2 billion valuation by Q3 2028. That trajectory is anchored in the Africa-wide $250 billion crypto opportunity, Nigeria's $92 billion market growing at 56% per annum, the dual-country regulatory moat, and bank-integrated access to more than 50 million users.

But the financial returns are the smaller story. The larger story is what this infrastructure enables. When KoinKoin is fully deployed, an entrepreneur in Accra can receive a stablecoin payment from a client in Abu Dhabi in seconds, at near-zero cost, with full regulatory compliance on both ends. A family in Lagos can send money to a relative in London without surrendering 10% to a legacy remittance corridor. A small business in Ghana can hold kGHS — a KoinKoin-issued stablecoin — as a hedge against FX volatility, without needing a bank account in New York.

These are not aspirational use cases. They are the product roadmap. And KoinKoin is the only platform on the continent with the regulatory permissions, the technical architecture, and the strategic partnerships to actually deliver them.

"We are not building a company. We are building the layer that every African company will one day run on. That is not ambition. That is the responsibility we have chosen to accept."

Ola Atose, Founder & Global CEO, KoinKoin

The $600 million Series A target for Q4 2027 is not theatre — it is the logical next station on a roadmap already in motion after eight years of honing, readiness and regulatory groundwork. The OTC desk is live. The compliance engine is built. The regulators are partners, not adversaries. The global-exchange on/off-ramp opportunity can transform volumes across both markets and convert regulatory normalisation into a durable early-mover advantage.

By Q3 2028, KoinKoin is targeting an almost $2 billion valuation because five years from now, KoinKoin will not merely be a platform — it will be infrastructure. Not a brand that people choose — infrastructure that powers the brands they use without even knowing. Like SWIFT, but built for Africa, by Africans, in the twenty-first century, at the speed that the twenty-first century demands.

And it started because one man refused to walk past a broken thing.

Africa.

The continent that taught the world what civilisation looked like
is about to teach it what financial infrastructure looks like
when it is built right, from the inside, by the people who live it.
